questions.question: Which data serialization format uses indentation and colons for key-value pairs without curly braces?
questions.explanation: YAML uses indentation and colons. JSON uses curly braces and quotes. XML uses angle-bracket tags. All three are used in network automation, but YAML is common in Ansible playbooks.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 57: Automation Overview; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 19

questions.question: Which network management protocol uses HTTPS with JSON/XML bodies (REST-style) to configure devices?
questions.explanation: RESTCONF uses standard HTTP(S) verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) with JSON or XML payloads. NETCONF uses SSH with XML-RPC. Both use YANG data models to describe device configuration.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 2, Chapter 19: Automation; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57

questions.question: What is the difference between a traditional network and an SDN (Software-Defined Networking) architecture?
options: SDN uses more physical hardware than traditional networks
SDN separates the control plane from the data plane — a centralized controller makes forwarding decisions
Traditional networks use APIs; SDN uses CLI only
SDN requires all devices to be from the same vendor
questions.explanation: In traditional networks, each device has both control plane (routing decisions) and data plane (packet forwarding) integrated. SDN centralizes the control plane into a controller (e.g., Cisco APIC, OpenDaylight). Devices just forward — the controller programs them via southbound APIs (OpenFlow, NETCONF).
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 34 Network Management; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 19

questions.question: Ansible uses which data format for playbooks, and what makes it agentless?
options: JSON playbooks; agentless because it uses a central database
XML playbooks; agentless because devices run a passive agent
YAML playbooks; agentless because it connects via SSH (no software needed on managed devices)
Python scripts; agentless because it uses UDP broadcasts
questions.explanation: Ansible uses YAML for playbooks (human-readable, indentation-based). It is agentless — it connects to managed devices using SSH (Linux) or WinRM (Windows), with no agent software needed on the target. For network devices, it uses NETCONF or specific Cisco/Arista/Juniper modules.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 57: Ansible, Puppet, Chef; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 19

questions.question: What does REST API stand for, and what HTTP methods does it commonly use?
options: Reliable Exchange State Transfer; uses CONNECT and SEND
Representational State Transfer; uses GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE
Remote Execution Session Transfer; uses FETCH and PUSH
Routed Exchange Synchronous Transport; uses REQUEST and RESPONSE
questions.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for APIs. It uses standard HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove). REST APIs are stateless and use JSON or XML for data exchange. Cisco DNA Center and other SDN controllers expose REST APIs.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: REST APIs; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is NETCONF and which transport protocol does it use?
options: A network monitoring protocol that uses UDP port 161
A network configuration protocol that uses SSH as its transport (port 830)
A routing protocol for SDN controllers
A telemetry protocol that uses gRPC
questions.explanation: NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol) is used to install, manipulate, and delete configurations on network devices. It runs over SSH (port 830) and uses XML for data encoding. NETCONF is a southbound API used by SDN controllers to configure network devices. It supports candidate, running, and startup datastores.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: NETCONF/RESTCONF; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the difference between Puppet and Ansible in terms of architecture?
options: Both are agent-based; Puppet uses Ruby, Ansible uses Python
Puppet uses an agent on managed devices (pull model); Ansible is agentless (push model via SSH)
Both are agentless; Puppet uses YAML, Ansible uses XML
Puppet uses SSH; Ansible uses NETCONF
questions.explanation: Puppet uses an AGENT installed on managed devices that periodically PULLS configuration from the Puppet master (pull model, Ruby-based). Ansible is AGENTLESS — it pushes configuration to devices via SSH (Linux) or WinRM (Windows) using YAML playbooks (push model, Python-based). Chef is also agent-based (pull model, Ruby).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: Ansible, Puppet, Chef; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: In software-defined networking (SDN), what is the role of southbound APIs?
options: To communicate with network operators and management systems (northbound)
To communicate between the SDN controller and network devices (data plane)
To authenticate users to the SDN controller
To translate between IPv4 and IPv6
questions.explanation: Southbound APIs (like OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF, gRPC) are used for communication between the SDN controller (control plane) and the network devices (data plane). Northbound APIs (REST, YANG) are used for communication between the controller and management applications/operators.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 54: SDN; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What data serialization format does RESTCONF use for transmitting configuration data?
questions.explanation: RESTCONF uses HTTP/HTTPS as transport and supports both XML and JSON for data encoding. It is built on top of YANG data models and uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE). NETCONF uses XML over SSH (port 830). RESTCONF runs over HTTPS (port 443).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: NETCONF/RESTCONF; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the purpose of YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) in network automation?
options: A scripting language for writing Ansible playbooks
A data modeling language that defines the structure of configuration and state data exchanged by NETCONF/RESTCONF
A GUI tool for SDN controller configuration
questions.explanation: YANG is a data modeling language used to describe the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and state data. YANG models are used by NETCONF and RESTCONF — they define what data can be configured (like a schema for network devices). Cisco IOS-XE, IOS-XR, and NX-OS all support YANG models.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: YANG; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What are the five standard HTTP methods used by REST APIs and what do they do?
options: GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove
questions.explanation: REST API HTTP methods: GET — retrieve resource (read-only, safe, idempotent). POST — create new resource. PUT — replace entire resource (idempotent). PATCH — partial update of resource. DELETE — remove resource. RESTCONF uses all these methods over HTTPS to manage network devices.

questions.question: What is NETCONF and what transport/port does it use?
options: A network configuration protocol using HTTPS/443 with JSON encoding
A network configuration protocol using SSH/830 with XML encoding and YANG data models
A SNMP replacement using UDP/830 with MIB data models
A REST-based API using HTTP/80 with XML or JSON
questions.explanation: NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol, RFC 6241) is a network management protocol that uses SSH on port 830 and XML for encoding. It uses YANG data models to describe network configuration and state. NETCONF operations include get-config, edit-config, copy-config, delete-config, lock, unlock, and close-session.

questions.question: Compare Ansible vs Puppet for network automation. Which is agentless and which is agent-based?
options: Both are agentless; Ansible uses SSH, Puppet uses HTTPS
Both require agents; Ansible uses YAML, Puppet uses Ruby DSL
questions.explanation: Ansible: agentless (no software installed on managed nodes), uses SSH/NETCONF to push configurations, uses YAML playbooks, simple to set up. Puppet: agent-based (Puppet agent installed on nodes), uses pull model (agents check in to Puppet master on TCP 8140), uses Ruby DSL manifests. Chef is also agent-based like Puppet.

questions.question: In an SDN architecture, what is the difference between the northbound and southbound APIs?
options: Northbound=between controller and network devices; Southbound=between controller and applications
Northbound=between controller and applications (REST); Southbound=between controller and network devices (OpenFlow/NETCONF)
Both connect to network devices; northbound uses TCP, southbound uses UDP
questions.explanation: SDN API hierarchy: Southbound APIs (also called device APIs) connect the SDN controller to the network devices it manages — examples include OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF, gRPC. Northbound APIs connect the controller to applications and management tools — typically REST APIs. The controller sits in the middle, translating intent (northbound) to device config (southbound).

questions.question: What is YANG and what is its relationship to NETCONF and RESTCONF?
options: YANG is a query language for XML databases used independently of NETCONF
YANG is a data modeling language that defines the structure of network configuration/state data; NETCONF and RESTCONF use YANG models to encode/validate data
YANG is a replacement for SNMP MIBs and only works with RESTCONF
YANG is a Cisco-proprietary scripting language for IOS automation
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation, RFC 6020/7950) is a data modeling language that defines the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and operational data. NETCONF uses YANG with XML encoding; RESTCONF uses YANG with XML or JSON encoding. YANG models specify exactly what configuration options exist and what constraints apply, enabling machine-readable network management.

questions.question: In traditional networking, the control plane and data plane are co-located on each device. What does SDN change about this?
options: SDN removes the data plane from routers/switches
SDN separates the control plane from the data plane — the control plane is centralized in an SDN controller
SDN moves the data plane to cloud servers while keeping control local
SDN combines both planes into a single software process
questions.explanation: Traditional devices have both control plane (routing protocols, STP, ARP) and data plane (forwarding based on lookup tables) co-located. SDN separates them: the control plane moves to a centralized controller, while network devices retain only the data plane (forwarding). This enables centralized policy, programmability, and dynamic topology management.

questions.question: What data format does RESTCONF use, and what transport does it run on?
options: XML only, runs over SSH port 830
XML or JSON, runs over HTTPS (TLS)
JSON only, runs over HTTP port 80
questions.explanation: RESTCONF (RFC 8040) is a REST-based protocol for network management. It uses HTTPS for transport (TLS-encrypted), and supports both XML and JSON as data encoding formats. It uses YANG data models (like NETCONF) but with RESTful HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) and standard URIs to address resources.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a network controller's 'intent-based networking' (IBN) capability?
options: Manually translates CLI commands to machine code
Allows administrators to declare WHAT the network should do (intent/policy), and the controller figures out HOW to implement it
Runs automated penetration tests against network devices
Provides real-time packet capture across the entire network
questions.explanation: Intent-Based Networking (IBN) abstracts network configuration: administrators define business outcomes or policies ('allow HR VLAN to access payroll server'), and the IBN controller translates this intent into specific device configurations automatically. This contrasts with traditional device-by-device CLI management. Cisco DNA Center is an example IBN controller.

questions.question: When using a REST API, which HTTP method is idempotent and used to completely replace an existing resource?
options: POST — creates or replaces any resource
GET — retrieves and caches for replacement
questions.explanation: PUT is idempotent (calling it multiple times produces the same result as calling it once) and completely replaces the target resource with the request body. POST is not idempotent (creates new resources each time). PATCH is for partial updates (modify specific fields). GET is read-only and safe. DELETE removes the resource.

questions.question: What is the difference between a traditional network device's control plane and data plane?
options: Control plane = hardware forwarding; Data plane = software routing decisions
Control plane = runs routing protocols and makes forwarding decisions; Data plane = actually forwards packets based on the FIB/CAM tables
Control plane = Layer 1-2 processing; Data plane = Layer 3-7 processing
They are the same thing on traditional devices
questions.explanation: Control plane: runs protocols (OSPF, STP, ARP) to build forwarding tables — CPU-intensive. Data plane (forwarding plane): uses the tables built by the control plane to forward packets/frames at line rate using specialized hardware (ASICs). Management plane: handles out-of-band management (SSH, SNMP). SDN separates these planes: control plane moves to a controller.

questions.question: What is Cisco SD-WAN (formerly Viptela) and what are its key components?
options: A MPLS WAN replacement using only ISP circuits
A software-defined WAN solution with vManage (management), vSmart (control), vBond (orchestration), and vEdge/cEdge (data plane)
A Cisco proprietary VPN technology for branch offices
A cloud-based load balancer for WAN traffic
questions.explanation: Cisco SD-WAN components: vManage — centralized NMS/GUI (management plane). vSmart — distributes policies/routes to vEdges (control plane). vBond — orchestrates initial connections, authenticates devices. vEdge/cEdge — physical/virtual WAN routers at sites (data plane). Benefits: centralized policy, transport independence (MPLS+internet+LTE), application-aware routing, lower cost.

questions.question: What is Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center) and what does it provide?
options: A Cisco cloud-only router management portal
An on-premises SDN controller providing intent-based networking, automation, assurance, and security policy for campus/enterprise networks
A RADIUS server replacement for AAA
A tool for converting Cisco IOS configs to YAML format
questions.explanation: Cisco Catalyst Center (DNA Center) is an on-premises SDN controller for enterprise campus networks that provides: (1) Design — network hierarchy, templates. (2) Policy — intent-based access policy (group-based with SGTs). (3) Provision — automated device configuration/onboarding (PnP). (4) Assurance — AI/ML-based network monitoring and troubleshooting. Uses Cisco SD-Access for network fabric.

questions.question: What is the primary management model for Cisco Meraki devices?
options: On-premises controller that you install in a data center
Cloud-managed — all configuration and monitoring done through the Meraki cloud dashboard
SNMP-only management through a separate NMS server
questions.explanation: Meraki uses a cloud-managed architecture: all devices (switches, APs, firewalls, cameras) are managed through the Meraki cloud dashboard. Zero-touch provisioning — devices call home to Meraki cloud when connected. No on-premises controller needed. Configuration, monitoring, firmware updates, and analytics all done through the cloud portal.

questions.question: When using Python for network automation, what does the `requests` library provide?
options: HTTP/HTTPS client functionality for making REST API calls
questions.explanation: The Python `requests` library is the standard HTTP client library used for REST API interactions. It supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE methods. When working with RESTCONF or other REST APIs, `requests` sends HTTP requests with appropriate headers (Content-Type: application/json), handles authentication, and parses responses. Example: `requests.get('https://device/restconf/data/...', auth=('user','pass'))`

questions.question: What is JSON and why is it commonly used in network automation REST APIs?
options: JavaScript Object Notation — a verbose XML alternative used only in web browsers
JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable data format using key-value pairs; easy to parse in Python with json.loads()/json.dumps()
A Cisco-proprietary data format for IOS configuration storage
A compiled binary format for efficient network data transmission
questions.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text-based data interchange format. It uses: objects {} (key-value pairs), arrays [] (ordered lists), strings, numbers, booleans, and null. REST APIs commonly return JSON. In Python: `json.loads(text)` parses JSON to dict, `json.dumps(dict)` converts to JSON. RESTCONF supports both JSON and XML. Compared to XML: shorter, easier to read.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and how does it relate to network automation?
options: Writing CLI commands in Python scripts to configure one device at a time

Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files (e.g., Terraform, Ansible) rather than manual processes — enabling version control, consistency, and rapid deployment
Converting router configurations to C++ code for faster processing
Using hardware description languages to program FPGA-based network devices
questions.explanation: IaC treats infrastructure configuration as software code: stored in version control (Git), peer-reviewed, tested, and deployed automatically. Benefits: consistency (no configuration drift), repeatability, rollback, scalability. Tools: Terraform (cloud infrastructure), Ansible (network configuration), Puppet/Chef. For networks: IaC means defining intended network state in YAML/JSON and having automation tools enforce it.

questions.question: What is the difference between version control (Git) and CI/CD in the context of network automation?
options: They are identical; CI/CD is just an older name for Git
Git = tracks changes to configuration files and enables collaboration/rollback; CI/CD = automates testing and deployment of those configurations
Git = deploys configurations; CI/CD = stores them
Git is for programmers only; CI/CD is network-specific
questions.explanation: Git (version control): tracks every change to configuration files, who made it, and when. Enables branching, merging, and rollback to any previous state. CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment): automates the pipeline from code commit to deployment. CI = automatically tests changes when committed. CD = automatically deploys passing changes. Together they enable reliable, auditable network configuration changes.

questions.question: Which HTTP status code indicates that a REST API request succeeded and a new resource was created?
questions.explanation: HTTP status codes: 200 = OK (successful GET/PUT/PATCH), 201 = Created (successful POST that created a resource), 204 = No Content (successful DELETE), 400 = Bad Request (malformed syntax), 401 = Unauthorized, 403 = Forbidden, 404 = Not Found, 500 = Internal Server Error.

questions.question: What is the difference between HTTP PUT and PATCH methods in a REST API?
options: PUT deletes a resource; PATCH creates it
PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH updates only specified fields
PUT is read-only; PATCH makes changes
questions.explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource with the provided data (idempotent full replace). PATCH partially updates an existing resource, modifying only the fields provided. GET retrieves data, POST creates new resources, DELETE removes resources. PUT and DELETE are idempotent (same result regardless of how many times called).

questions.question: Which of the following best describes Ansible's architecture?
options: Agent-based; requires an agent installed on each managed node
Agentless; uses SSH (or APIs) to push configurations from a control node
Pull-based; managed nodes pull configs from a server
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless — no software needs to be installed on managed devices. It uses SSH (or APIs for network devices) to push configurations from the control node. It uses YAML playbooks and an inventory file. Puppet uses agents (pull-based, TCP 8140). Chef also uses agents. Ansible is simplest to set up.

questions.question: How does Puppet manage device configurations?
options: Agentless SSH push from control node
Agent-based pull: agents request catalogs from the Puppet master
questions.explanation: Puppet uses an agent-based pull model: agents installed on managed nodes periodically pull configuration catalogs from the Puppet master server. Configurations are written in Puppet's Ruby-based DSL (manifests). Communication uses TCP 8140. This is the opposite of Ansible's agentless push model.

questions.question: Which transport protocol and port does NETCONF use?
questions.explanation: NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol) uses SSH over TCP port 830. It uses XML for data encoding and YANG for data modeling. NETCONF operations include get, get-config, edit-config, and commit. RESTCONF is a REST-based alternative that runs over HTTPS and supports both XML and JSON.

questions.question: What is the purpose of YANG in network automation?
options: A scripting language for writing automation playbooks
A data modeling language that defines the structure of network configuration and state data
A network protocol for delivering configurations to devices
A GUI tool for managing network automation workflows
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) is a data modeling language used to define the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and operational data. YANG models are used by both NETCONF and RESTCONF to describe what data is available on a device. Think of YANG as the schema that defines what you can GET or SET.

questions.question: In SDN architecture, what does the northbound API connect?
options: The SDN controller to physical network devices
The SDN controller to applications/orchestration systems
The data plane to the control plane
questions.explanation: SDN APIs: Northbound APIs (e.g., REST) connect the SDN controller to applications and orchestration systems (north = toward applications). Southbound APIs (e.g., OpenFlow, NETCONF) connect the controller to the network devices (south = toward infrastructure). The controller provides a centralized view of the entire network.

questions.question: Which of the following is valid JSON syntax?
questions.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) uses double-quoted strings as keys and values, with objects in curly braces {} and arrays in square brackets []. Option A is XML. Option B is JavaScript object literal notation (not valid JSON — keys must be quoted). Option D uses TOML/INI-style syntax. JSON is used by RESTCONF and REST APIs.

questions.question: What is Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) primarily used for?
options: Intent-based networking: automated network management, assurance, and policy enforcement
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center (now Catalyst Center) is Cisco's Intent-Based Networking (IBN) controller for on-premises enterprise networks. It provides: network automation (provision devices via GUI or API), assurance (AI/ML analytics), security policy (group-based access), and SD-Access fabric management. It uses REST APIs for integration.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Compiling network device firmware from source code
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through version-controlled machine-readable definition files
Using CLI scripts to configure devices manually
Converting network diagrams to configuration files
questions.explanation: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) manages infrastructure (networks, servers, etc.) through declarative or procedural definition files stored in version control (e.g., Git). This enables consistent, repeatable provisioning, automated testing, and rollback. Tools like Terraform, Ansible, and Puppet implement IaC principles.

questions.question: In Cisco SD-WAN, what is the role of vManage?
options: Data plane — forwards actual traffic between sites
Centralized management plane — provides GUI and APIs for configuration, monitoring
Orchestration plane — authenticates and onboards new devices
Control plane — distributes routing and policy information
questions.explanation: Cisco SD-WAN components: vManage = centralized management GUI/API (NMS), vSmart = control plane (distributes policies/routes), vBond = orchestration (authenticates new devices), vEdge/cEdge = data plane (edge routers). The management, control, and data planes are separated in SD-WAN.

questions.question: What Python library is commonly used to make HTTP/REST API calls to network devices?
questions.explanation: The `requests` library is the standard Python library for HTTP/REST API calls. It supports GET, POST, PUT, DELETE with simple syntax. `paramiko` is for SSH connections. `netmiko` is built on paramiko for multi-vendor SSH CLI automation. `ncclient` is for NETCONF. For REST APIs (RESTCONF, DNA Center), `requests` is the go-to library.

questions.question: How does RESTCONF differ from NETCONF?
options: RESTCONF uses XML only; NETCONF uses JSON only
RESTCONF uses HTTP/HTTPS methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE); NETCONF uses SSH with XML RPCs
RESTCONF is for physical devices; NETCONF is for virtual
They are identical protocols with different names
questions.explanation: RESTCONF (RFC 8040) maps NETCONF operations to REST-style HTTP methods over HTTPS. It supports both JSON and XML. NETCONF uses SSH (port 830) with XML-encoded RPCs. Both use YANG data models. RESTCONF is easier to integrate with web applications; NETCONF provides full transaction support with commit/rollback.

questions.question: In traditional networking, what is the 'data plane'?
options: The management plane where administrators configure devices
The control plane where routing protocols run
The forwarding plane that actually moves packets based on the forwarding table
The application plane where network services run
questions.explanation: Network planes: Control plane = routing protocols, STP, ARP — decides where traffic goes. Data plane (forwarding plane) = actually forwards packets using the FIB/CAM table built by the control plane. Management plane = out-of-band management (SSH, SNMP, syslog). In SDN, the control plane is centralized in the controller, separating it from the distributed data plane.

questions.question: What is a hypervisor and what types exist?
options: A network device that virtualizes VLANs; Type 1 is hardware, Type 2 is software
Software that creates/manages VMs; Type 1 runs on bare metal, Type 2 runs on top of a host OS
A software-defined network controller; Type 1 is centralized, Type 2 is distributed
A container orchestration platform; Type 1 uses Docker, Type 2 uses Kubernetes
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (VMM) creates and manages virtual machines. Type 1 (bare-metal): runs directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM) — enterprise grade, more efficient. Type 2 (hosted): runs on top of a host OS (VMware Workstation, VirtualBox) — for desktop use. VMs enable server consolidation, rapid provisioning, and cloud computing.

questions.question: Which cloud service model gives customers the MOST control over the underlying infrastructure?
questions.explanation: IaaS gives the most customer control: customers manage OS, applications, middleware, and data. The provider manages physical hardware, networking, and virtualization. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute. PaaS: provider also manages OS and runtime (customer manages apps/data). SaaS: provider manages everything (customer only uses the app, e.g., Office 365, Salesforce).

questions.question: Why is Git important in network automation?
options: Git is a network protocol for device configuration backups
Git provides version control for automation scripts and configuration files, enabling rollback and collaboration
Git replaces NETCONF for device configuration management
Git is a monitoring tool for tracking network performance
questions.explanation: Git is a distributed version control system used in network automation to: track changes to playbooks, scripts, and config files; enable rollback to previous working versions; allow collaboration through branching/merging; integrate with CI/CD pipelines for automated testing and deployment. Infrastructure as Code relies heavily on Git.

questions.question: Which data format does NETCONF use, and which does RESTCONF support?
options: NETCONF uses JSON; RESTCONF uses XML
NETCONF uses XML; RESTCONF supports both XML and JSON
NETCONF uses YAML; RESTCONF uses JSON
questions.explanation: NETCONF uses XML exclusively for all data encoding. RESTCONF (the HTTP-based alternative) supports both XML and JSON, with JSON being more popular due to its lighter syntax and better Python integration. Ansible playbooks use YAML. Python's `json` and `xml.etree` libraries handle these formats respectively.

questions.question: What is the key characteristic of Cisco Meraki compared to traditional network management?
options: Meraki uses NETCONF for all device configuration
Meraki devices are managed entirely through a cloud-based dashboard — no CLI required
Meraki is an on-premises controller requiring local server installation
Meraki only supports wireless LAN management
questions.explanation: Cisco Meraki is a cloud-managed networking solution where all configuration is done through a centralized cloud dashboard (dashboard.meraki.com). There is no CLI — all switches, APs, routers, and firewalls are configured via the web GUI or Meraki APIs. Devices phone home to the Meraki cloud for configuration. Ideal for distributed deployments without on-site IT.

questions.question: What role does OpenFlow play in SDN?
options: It is a northbound API used by applications to communicate with the SDN controller
It is a southbound API protocol that allows the SDN controller to program the flow tables in network devices
It is the data modeling language used by SDN controllers
It is a network monitoring protocol for SDN environments
questions.explanation: OpenFlow is a southbound API protocol in SDN that enables the SDN controller to directly manage the flow tables (forwarding rules) in switches and routers. The controller tells devices exactly how to forward specific flows. OpenFlow was pioneered by Stanford and the Open Networking Foundation. Other southbound APIs include NETCONF and OVSDB.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key benefit of network automation over manual CLI configuration?
options: Automation is faster but less accurate than manual configuration
Automation eliminates the need for network engineers
Automation provides consistency, repeatability, and faster deployment with fewer human errors
Automation only works on Cisco devices
questions.explanation: Network automation benefits: consistency (same config every time), speed (deploy to hundreds of devices simultaneously), reduced human errors, version control (track changes via Git), scalability (no linear increase in work for more devices), and faster troubleshooting through correlation. Engineers shift from CLI work to automation design.

questions.question: In Ansible, what is a 'playbook'?
options: A script for configuring individual devices one at a time
A YAML file defining automation tasks and the hosts to run them on
An inventory file listing all managed devices
A Python script using the Ansible API
questions.explanation: Ansible playbooks are YAML files that define: which hosts to manage (from inventory), which tasks to execute (using modules like `ios_config`, `uri`, etc.), variables, and handlers. Playbooks are human-readable and idempotent. An inventory file lists managed hosts/groups. Modules are the units of work (e.g., `cisco.ios.ios_command` for Cisco IOS).

questions.question: What is Cisco SD-Access?
options: Software-Defined Wide Area Network for branch connectivity
An intent-based campus fabric solution using LISP and VXLAN for endpoint segmentation
A cloud-based access control solution for SaaS applications
A next-generation firewall platform for campus security
questions.explanation: Cisco SD-Access (Software-Defined Access) is a campus fabric solution managed by DNA Center (Catalyst Center). It uses LISP for control plane (endpoint-to-location mapping), VXLAN for data plane encapsulation, and Cisco TrustSec for policy/segmentation. It enables macro-segmentation (VRFs) and micro-segmentation (SGTs) with automated policy enforcement.

questions.question: What is the difference between declarative and imperative configuration management?
options: Declarative = step-by-step commands; Imperative = defines desired end state
Declarative = defines desired end state; Imperative = step-by-step how to achieve it
They are identical in outcome but differ in syntax
Declarative is used by Ansible; Imperative is used by Python scripts only
questions.explanation: Declarative = describe the desired end state, let the tool figure out how to get there (Ansible, Puppet, Terraform). Imperative = specify exact steps to execute (Python scripts, CLI). Declarative is generally preferred for idempotency (applying the same configuration multiple times has the same result). YANG models are declarative.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a YANG module container element?
options: A container groups related YANG nodes (similar to a JSON object or XML element)
A container defines the allowable range for a numeric value
A container specifies which devices support a particular feature
A container encapsulates NETCONF RPC operations
questions.explanation: YANG data modeling elements: Container = groups related child nodes (no value itself, like a JSON object). Leaf = holds a single value. Leaf-list = multiple values of the same type (like a JSON array of scalars). List = multiple instances of a structure (like a JSON array of objects). Module = the root of a YANG definition file.

questions.question: What does it mean for a REST API to be 'stateless'?
options: The API never returns error codes
Each request contains all information needed; the server does not maintain session state between requests
The API only supports read operations (GET)
REST APIs cannot maintain persistent connections
questions.explanation: REST is stateless: each HTTP request must contain all information needed (authentication, parameters, etc.) — the server does not store session state between requests. Benefits: scalability (any server can handle any request), reliability (no session sync needed). Authentication is typically via tokens (Bearer/JWT) or API keys included in each request header.

questions.question: What is the difference between a virtual machine (VM) and a container?
options: VMs are faster; containers are more secure
VMs virtualize the entire OS including kernel; containers share the host OS kernel and isolate processes
Containers require a hypervisor; VMs do not
They are identical technologies with different marketing names
questions.explanation: VMs: each has its own complete OS, kernel, and application — heavy (GBs), full isolation, managed by hypervisor. Containers (Docker, etc.): share the host OS kernel, isolate application processes using namespaces/cgroups — lightweight (MBs), start in seconds, less isolation. VMs: better security isolation. Containers: faster, more portable, better for microservices. Kubernetes orchestrates containers.

questions.question: What type of API does Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) expose for automation?
options: REST APIs over HTTPS (northbound), with southbound to devices via NETCONF/SNMP/CLI
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center exposes northbound REST APIs over HTTPS for integration with external applications, orchestration, and custom automation. Southbound, DNA Center communicates with network devices using NETCONF, SNMP, CLI (SSH/Telnet), and other protocols. The Intent API is the primary programmability interface for DNA Center.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT an example of network automation tooling?
questions.explanation: Wireshark is a packet capture/analysis tool, not an automation tool. Ansible, Terraform, Chef, Puppet, and SaltStack are configuration management/automation tools. Nornir is a Python automation framework. Netmiko/NAPALM are Python libraries for network device automation. For the CCNA exam, focus on Ansible, Puppet, and Python.

questions.question: Where can you find standard YANG models for network devices?
options: Proprietary: each vendor creates their own with no standard
IETF and OpenConfig publish standard models; vendors also publish device-specific models
YANG models are embedded in IOS and not publicly available
questions.explanation: YANG model sources: IETF publishes standard models (RFC-based, e.g., ietf-interfaces.yang). OpenConfig publishes vendor-neutral models for common features (BGP, interfaces, QoS). Individual vendors (Cisco, Juniper, etc.) publish device-specific native models with proprietary features. Tools like YANG Suite and pyang help browse and validate YANG models. GitHub: YangModels/yang repository.

questions.question: What is idempotency in network automation?
options: The ability to run automation scripts in parallel
Applying the same automation task multiple times produces the same result as applying it once
The guarantee that automation scripts never fail
Using different tools for different automation tasks
questions.explanation: Idempotency means running the same operation N times gives the same result as running it once. Example: an Ansible task that sets `ip address 10.1.1.1/24` on an interface — running it 5 times won't cause problems; the result is always the same. Declarative tools (Ansible, Puppet, Terraform) are generally idempotent. Non-idempotent: a script that appends a line to a config file each time it runs.

questions.question: What is model-driven telemetry and how does it differ from traditional SNMP polling?
options: They are identical; model-driven telemetry is just the new name for SNMP
Telemetry pushes data from devices to a collector continuously; SNMP polling requires the manager to request data periodically
SNMP provides real-time data; telemetry only provides historical data
Telemetry uses UDP; SNMP uses TCP
questions.explanation: Model-driven telemetry (MDT) uses a push model: devices stream data to a collector using YANG models and protocols like gRPC/gNMI. More scalable and timely than SNMP's pull model (manager must poll each device). Supports subscription-based updates (periodic or on-change). Lower overhead and faster anomaly detection. Used by Cisco IOS XE/XR with gNMI/NETCONF streaming.

questions.question: What is Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP)?
options: A security feature that prevents unauthorized configuration changes
Automatic device provisioning without manual CLI interaction, typically using DHCP + TFTP/HTTP to fetch configs
A wireless deployment method for zero RF interference
A Cisco proprietary VLAN provisioning protocol
questions.explanation: ZTP (Zero-Touch Provisioning) allows new devices to be automatically configured when first powered on. Process: device boots → DHCP assigns IP + option 67 (config file URL) → device fetches config from TFTP/HTTP/HTTPs server → runs the config. No manual CLI needed. Cisco PnP (Plug-and-Play) is Cisco's ZTP solution integrated with DNA Center. Reduces deployment time for large branch rollouts.

questions.question: What Python library is commonly used for SSH-based CLI automation of network devices?
questions.explanation: Netmiko is a multi-vendor SSH library built on Paramiko, designed specifically for network devices. It handles the quirks of network device SSH (prompts, pagination, terminal length). Supports Cisco IOS, NXOS, ASA, Juniper, Arista, etc. `requests` is for HTTP/REST APIs. `ncclient` is for NETCONF. `boto3` is for AWS. `flask` is a web framework.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid use case for network automation?
options: Replacing routers and switches with software
Automatically provisioning ACLs on new branch routers using a template
Eliminating the need for network monitoring
Removing the need for IP addressing on devices
questions.explanation: Network automation use cases: mass configuration changes (update NTP servers on all routers), compliance checking (verify all devices have correct ACLs/banners), automated provisioning (new branch router gets standard config from template), software upgrades, configuration backup, network discovery/documentation. Automation doesn't replace devices — it manages them more efficiently.

questions.question: What NETCONF operation retrieves the running configuration from a device?
questions.explanation: NETCONF operations: <get-config> = retrieve configuration datastore (source: running, startup, candidate). <get> = retrieve config AND operational state data. <edit-config> = modify configuration. <commit> = commit candidate config to running. <copy-config> = copy one datastore to another. <delete-config> = delete a datastore. <lock>/<unlock> = prevent concurrent edits.

questions.question: What does CI/CD stand for in the context of network automation?
options: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment — automated testing and delivery pipeline for code changes
questions.explanation: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment) in network automation: CI = automatically test changes when committed to Git (syntax validation, unit tests, network simulation). CD = automatically deploy validated changes to production devices. Pipeline: code commit → lint/syntax check → integration test → staging deploy → production deploy. Reduces human error and accelerates change cycles. Tools: GitLab CI, Jenkins, GitHub Actions.

questions.question: What type of automation tool is Terraform?
options: A configuration management tool like Ansible for device configuration
An Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool primarily used for provisioning cloud infrastructure
A network monitoring and alerting platform
questions.explanation: Terraform (HashiCorp) is an IaC tool for provisioning infrastructure — it creates/manages resources declaratively using HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language). Strong for cloud provisioning (AWS/Azure/GCP VMs, networks, load balancers). Uses state files to track what it manages. Ansible is better for configuration management after provisioning. Together: Terraform provisions, Ansible configures.

questions.question: What is gNMI (gRPC Network Management Interface) used for?
options: A CLI scripting language for Cisco IOS automation
A network management protocol using gRPC for real-time telemetry and configuration management
A GUI tool for visualizing network topologies
questions.explanation: gNMI uses gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) over HTTP/2 for network management. Supports: Get (retrieve config/state), Set (modify config), Subscribe (streaming telemetry/notifications). More efficient than NETCONF for high-frequency telemetry. Used by OpenConfig and supported by modern Cisco IOS XE/XR, Juniper, Arista. Part of model-driven streaming telemetry architecture.

questions.question: What is Cisco Modeling Labs (CML) / VIRL used for?
options: A network simulation/emulation platform for testing configurations before deploying to production
A monitoring tool for production network devices
questions.explanation: Cisco Modeling Labs (CML, formerly VIRL) is a network emulation platform that runs actual Cisco IOS/NX-OS/IOS-XE images in a virtual environment. Used for: testing configurations, learning labs, pre-deployment validation, automation testing. Other popular simulators: GNS3 (open-source, runs Cisco images with IOU/QEMU), Packet Tracer (Cisco learning tool, simplified models). Virtual devices support NETCONF/gNMI for automation testing.

questions.question: What is NAPALM in the context of network automation?
options: Network Automation and Programmability Abstraction Layer with Multivendor support — Python library for config management
A Cisco proprietary configuration deployment tool
A YANG data model for network address plan management
questions.explanation: NAPALM (Network Automation and Programmability Abstraction Layer with Multivendor support) is a Python library that provides a unified API for interacting with multiple network OS types (Cisco IOS, NX-OS, EOS, Junos). Functions: get_interfaces(), get_bgp_neighbors(), load_merge_candidate(), compare_config(), commit_config(). Simplifies multi-vendor automation. Integrates with Ansible and Salt.

questions.question: What is the difference between an on-box and off-box automation approach?
options: On-box is more secure; off-box is faster
On-box: automation logic runs directly on the network device (Python/EEM); Off-box: logic runs on a separate controller/server that manages devices
On-box uses CLI; off-box uses REST APIs exclusively
They are identical approaches with different vendor names
questions.explanation: On-box automation: scripts run directly on the device (Cisco Guest Shell, EEM applets, Python on IOS XE). Immediate reaction to local events, no external dependency. Off-box automation: logic runs on external systems (Ansible control node, Python scripts, DNA Center, NSO). Greater scalability, version control, centralized management. Most enterprise automation is off-box for maintainability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a network 'golden configuration' template in automation?
options: A configuration that has been backed up to a TFTP server
A standard, approved baseline configuration applied consistently to all devices of the same type
A configuration with maximum security settings regardless of use case
The most recently deployed configuration on any device
questions.explanation: A golden configuration template defines the standard baseline for device types (edge router, core switch, access AP). It includes security hardening, standard services, naming conventions, and management settings. Automation ensures all devices conform to the golden template. Drift detection compares current device config to the template and alerts on or automatically corrects deviations. Key to compliance and consistency.

questions.question: How do REST APIs typically handle authentication?
options: Using API keys, OAuth tokens, or Basic Auth (username:password encoded in Base64) in HTTP headers
questions.explanation: REST API authentication methods: API Keys (static token in header or query param). Basic Auth (Base64-encoded username:password in Authorization header — insecure without TLS). OAuth 2.0 / Bearer tokens (token obtained from auth server, included as 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'). JWT (JSON Web Tokens, signed). Cisco DNA Center uses token-based auth: POST to /dna/system/api/v1/auth/token with Basic Auth to get a token, then use token for subsequent calls.

questions.question: What is Cisco NSO (Network Services Orchestrator)?
options: A monitoring platform for Cisco Meraki cloud networks
A network orchestration platform that abstracts device configuration using YANG models and NEDs
A next-generation firewall for service provider edge
A wireless LAN controller for enterprise deployments
questions.explanation: Cisco NSO (formerly Tail-f NCS) is an enterprise network orchestrator that uses YANG models and NEDs (Network Element Drivers) to provide a device-agnostic abstraction layer. NSO can configure multi-vendor devices using a single northbound API. Supports NETCONF natively, with CLI-based NEDs for devices without NETCONF. Used for service provisioning, compliance checking, and closed-loop automation in service providers and large enterprises.

questions.question: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data from a REST API without modifying it?
questions.explanation: GET is the idempotent read-only HTTP method. POST creates resources, PUT replaces them, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes them.

questions.question: Which HTTP status code indicates a successfully created resource in a REST API?
questions.explanation: 201 Created is returned when a POST request successfully creates a new resource. 200 OK is general success. 204 is success with no content returned.

questions.question: What is the correct JSON data type for representing a list of items?
questions.explanation: JSON arrays use square brackets []. Objects use curly braces {}. JSON supports strings, numbers, booleans, null, objects, and arrays.

questions.question: What transport protocol and port does NETCONF use?
questions.explanation: NETCONF uses SSH on port 830. It encodes data in XML and uses YANG data models for structured configuration management.

questions.question: What is RESTCONF?
options: An HTTP-based protocol using REST principles to manage YANG-modeled network devices
A Python library for SSH automation
questions.explanation: RESTCONF (RFC 8040) uses HTTP/HTTPS with REST semantics (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) to manipulate YANG-modeled data. It supports both XML and JSON encoding.

questions.question: What is YANG in the context of network automation?
options: A data modeling language used with NETCONF and RESTCONF
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) is a data modeling language (RFC 6020/7950) that defines the structure of configuration and state data. NETCONF and RESTCONF use YANG models.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes Ansible?
options: Requires an agent on each managed device
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless — it uses SSH (or WinRM for Windows) to push configurations. No agent software is needed on managed devices. Playbooks are written in YAML.

questions.question: Which configuration management tool uses a pull model with agents running on managed nodes?
questions.explanation: Puppet uses an agent-based pull model. Agents on managed nodes periodically pull configuration manifests from the Puppet master (TCP 8140). Written in Ruby.

questions.question: In SDN architecture, what is the northbound interface?
options: The interface between the SDN controller and network devices
The interface between applications and the SDN controller
The physical port on the controller
questions.explanation: Northbound interfaces (typically REST APIs) allow applications to communicate with the SDN controller. Southbound interfaces (OpenFlow, NETCONF) connect the controller to network devices.

questions.question: What is the role of Cisco DNA Center in network management?
options: It provides CLI-only management for IOS-XE devices
It is an Intent-Based Networking platform with REST APIs for automated configuration
It replaces OSPF as a routing protocol
It is an open-source SDN controller
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center is an IBN (Intent-Based Networking) platform that provides a GUI and REST APIs for automated network provisioning, assurance, and management across the underlay and overlay.

questions.question: Which cloud service model gives users control over the OS, middleware, and applications but not the underlying hardware?
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized compute, storage, and networking. Users manage the OS and above. PaaS provides a platform; SaaS provides just the application.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of a public cloud deployment model?
options: Resources are dedicated to a single organization
Resources are shared among multiple tenants and managed by a provider
Resources are split between on-premises and public cloud
questions.explanation: Public cloud resources (AWS, Azure, GCP) are shared multi-tenant infrastructure managed by a third-party provider and accessible over the Internet.

questions.question: What is the advantage of model-driven telemetry (gNMI/gRPC) over traditional SNMP polling?
options: Lower CPU usage on the management station
Push-based streaming provides real-time data without polling delays
questions.explanation: Model-driven telemetry streams data from devices to collectors in real time (push model). SNMP polling is periodic (pull), which can miss rapid changes and add latency.

questions.question: What four main functional areas does Cisco DNA Center provide?
questions.explanation: DNA Center's four pillars: Design (network hierarchy/settings), Policy (group-based access), Provision (automated deployment), and Assurance (health monitoring/AI analytics).

questions.question: Which Cisco solution provides cloud-managed networking with no on-premises controller?
questions.explanation: Cisco Meraki is a cloud-managed platform where all configuration and monitoring is done through the Meraki dashboard in the cloud. No physical controller hardware is needed on-premises.

questions.question: What does 'idempotency' mean in the context of network automation?
options: Running the same automation task multiple times always produces the same result
Automation scripts run automatically without intervention
questions.explanation: An idempotent operation produces the same result regardless of how many times it's applied. If a device already has the correct config, re-running the script makes no changes — prevents config drift.

questions.question: What is a Terraform use case in network infrastructure?
options: CLI automation on Cisco IOS devices
Provisioning cloud infrastructure (VMs, VPCs, subnets) using declarative HCL code
questions.explanation: Terraform is an IaC (Infrastructure as Code) tool using HCL. It declaratively defines and provisions cloud resources (AWS, Azure, GCP). State files track what's deployed.

questions.question: What Python library is commonly used for SSH-based CLI automation on network devices?
questions.explanation: Netmiko is a Python library that simplifies SSH connections to multi-vendor network devices, handling prompts, scrolling, and connection management. Built on Paramiko.

questions.question: Which Cisco SD-WAN component is responsible for centralized policy management and route control?
questions.explanation: vSmart is the SD-WAN controller that manages routing policies and pushes them to vEdge routers via OMP. vManage is the GUI. vBond orchestrates initial authentication.

questions.question: What REST API HTTP method would you use to partially update an existing resource?
questions.explanation: PATCH partially updates a resource (only the specified fields). PUT replaces the entire resource. POST creates a new resource. DELETE removes it.

questions.question: What is Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP)?
options: A method to provision devices without physical console access by automatically loading configs on first boot
A CLI tool for bulk configuration
A Cisco IOS command for factory reset
questions.explanation: ZTP allows new network devices to automatically download and apply a configuration when they first boot, with no human intervention. The device uses DHCP to find a provisioning server (HTTP/TFTP).

questions.question: What is the difference between configuration management (Ansible/Puppet) and IaC (Terraform)?
options: Config management configures software/OS on existing hosts; IaC provisions the infrastructure (VMs, networks) itself
Terraform is for on-premises only; Ansible is cloud-only
IaC uses YAML; config management uses HCL
questions.explanation: Configuration management (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) manages software/settings on existing hosts. IaC tools (Terraform) provision the infrastructure (VMs, VPCs, load balancers) from scratch.

questions.question: What does 'stateless' mean in the context of REST APIs?
options: Each API request contains all information needed — the server maintains no client session state between requests
REST APIs cannot handle stateful sessions
Stateless means no authentication is used
questions.explanation: REST is stateless — each HTTP request is independent and self-contained. The server doesn't store session state. Authentication tokens/credentials must be included in each request.

questions.question: What is CI/CD in the context of network automation?
options: Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery — automated testing and deployment pipeline for configuration changes
questions.explanation: CI/CD pipelines automate testing of configuration changes (CI) and their deployment to devices (CD). This enables safe, frequent, and consistent network changes with automated rollback on failure.

questions.question: What are the four NETCONF operations (RPC calls)?
questions.explanation: Core NETCONF operations: get-config (read config datastore), edit-config (modify config), copy-config (copy between datastores), delete-config (delete a datastore). Also: get (state+config), lock, unlock.

questions.question: What is a hybrid cloud deployment model?
options: A cloud used exclusively by one company on-premises
A combination of private on-premises infrastructure and public cloud, integrated together
A cloud managed by multiple companies
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private (on-premises) and public cloud resources, allowing data/applications to move between them. Enables 'cloud bursting' for peak demand while keeping sensitive data on-premises.

questions.question: What is a golden configuration template in network automation?
options: A configuration saved only on gold-tier devices
A master configuration blueprint used to ensure consistent, compliant configurations across all devices
A Cisco DNA Center feature only
questions.explanation: A golden config template defines the standard, approved configuration for a device type. Automation tools use it to provision new devices consistently and to detect/remediate configuration drift.

questions.question: What three security services does TLS provide?
questions.explanation: TLS provides: Authentication (certificate-based server/mutual auth), Encryption (AES), and Integrity (HMAC). These three together ensure secure communications over HTTPS, SMTP, etc.

questions.question: When using REST APIs for network automation, which authentication method provides the most granular access control?
options: OAuth 2.0 with bearer tokens and scoped permissions
questions.explanation: OAuth 2.0 with bearer tokens allows fine-grained scopes (read-only, specific resources), token expiry, and revocation without exposing credentials. Basic auth is simple but less secure. API keys lack per-user granularity.

questions.question: What is a CRL (Certificate Revocation List) used for in PKI?
options: A list of trusted root CAs
A list of certificates that have been revoked before their expiry date
A list of clients with certificates
A catalog of cipher suites supported by the CA
questions.explanation: A CRL contains serial numbers of certificates revoked by the CA (due to key compromise, employee departure, etc.). Clients check the CRL or OCSP to verify a certificate hasn't been revoked before trusting it.

questions.question: What is a YANG 'leaf' node?
options: A container that holds other nodes
A single data value (string, integer, boolean) with no child nodes
A list of instances with keys
questions.explanation: In YANG: leaf = single value node (like a variable). container = grouping of related leaves. list = repeating set of entries identified by a key. module = the top-level YANG file.

questions.question: What is configuration drift and how does automation address it?
options: When IOS versions differ between devices
When device configurations deviate from the desired/golden state due to manual changes; automation detects and remediates this automatically
questions.explanation: Configuration drift occurs when individual manual changes accumulate, causing devices to diverge from the intended configuration. Automation tools continuously validate and enforce the golden config, preventing/remediating drift.

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 404 mean in a REST API response?
questions.explanation: 404 Not Found = the requested resource doesn't exist. Common REST status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.

questions.question: What is OpenFlow in SDN?
options: A southbound protocol that allows an SDN controller to program flow tables in switches
A northbound REST API for applications
questions.explanation: OpenFlow is a southbound protocol that enables an SDN controller to add, modify, or delete flow entries in a switch's flow table, decoupling the control plane from the data plane.

questions.question: Which approach describes 'off-box' automation?
options: Scripts running directly on the network device's CLI
Automation scripts running on an external system (server/workstation) that communicate with devices via APIs or SSH
Using SNMP MIBs to configure devices
Configuring devices through a web browser
questions.explanation: Off-box automation runs on an external controller/server using Python, Ansible, or similar tools that communicate with devices via SSH, NETCONF, or REST APIs. On-box automation runs scripts directly on the device (e.g., Cisco EEM, Guest Shell).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cisco EEM (Embedded Event Manager)?
options: An on-box automation tool that monitors events and triggers TCL/CLI scripts in response
questions.explanation: Cisco EEM is an on-box automation feature that detects events (syslog messages, interface up/down, CPU thresholds) and triggers applets (CLI scripts) in response — without an external controller.

questions.question: What file format are Ansible playbooks written in?
questions.explanation: Ansible playbooks use YAML (Yet Another Markup Language) format — human-readable, indentation-based syntax. Each playbook contains plays which define tasks to execute on hosts.

questions.question: What is an Ansible inventory file?
options: A list of installed Python packages
A file listing the managed hosts and groups that Ansible playbooks target
questions.explanation: The Ansible inventory file (hosts file) defines the managed nodes (hostnames/IPs) organized into groups. Playbooks target specific hosts or groups from this inventory.

questions.question: What protocol does gRPC/gNMI use for transport?
questions.explanation: gRPC uses HTTP/2 as transport with TLS for encryption. HTTP/2 enables multiplexed streams and bidirectional communication — key for efficient streaming telemetry from network devices.

questions.question: What is SaaS (Software as a Service)?
options: Provides virtualized compute and storage infrastructure
Provides a development platform and runtime environment
Delivers fully managed applications over the internet — user manages nothing but their data and access
questions.explanation: SaaS delivers complete applications over the internet (Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Webex). Users access the software via browser — no OS, middleware, or infrastructure management required.

questions.question: What is the core principle of Zero Trust security architecture?
options: Trust all internal network traffic by default
Verify every user and device explicitly before granting minimum-required access, regardless of location
Block all external traffic and allow all internal traffic
Use perimeter firewalls as the only security control
questions.explanation: Zero Trust assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated. Principle of least privilege is applied to all users, devices, and services.

questions.question: Which type of social engineering attack uses email to deceive users into revealing credentials or installing malware?
questions.explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails that appear legitimate to trick users. Spear phishing targets specific individuals. Vishing=voice/phone, Smishing=SMS/text, Whaling=targets executives.

questions.question: Which RADIUS attribute separates authentication and authorization in RADIUS accounting?
options: RADIUS uses separate ports for each function: UDP 1812 (auth/authz), UDP 1813 (accounting)
RADIUS combines all three AAA functions in one packet type
RADIUS only supports authentication, not authorization
questions.explanation: RADIUS separates authentication/authorization (UDP 1812) from accounting (UDP 1813). In practice, RADIUS combines authentication and authorization in the Access-Accept response (which also returns authorization attributes).

questions.question: How does RESTCONF differ from NETCONF in terms of transport and encoding?
options: RESTCONF uses SSH/XML; NETCONF uses HTTPS/JSON
RESTCONF uses HTTPS with REST semantics and supports JSON or XML; NETCONF uses SSH with XML only
RESTCONF is for IPv6 only; NETCONF is for IPv4
questions.explanation: NETCONF: SSH/830, XML encoding only, RPC-based operations. RESTCONF: HTTPS, REST semantics (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), supports both XML and JSON. Both use YANG data models.

questions.question: What Cisco DNA Center feature uses AI/ML to automatically identify network issues?
questions.explanation: DNA Center Assurance uses AI/ML analytics to monitor network health, identify root causes of issues, and proactively notify administrators — providing a 360-degree view of devices, clients, and applications.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using version control (e.g., Git) for network configurations?
options: Complete history of changes, ability to roll back to known-good state, and collaboration on configuration changes
questions.explanation: Git version control provides: full change history, diff between versions, rollback capability, branching for testing, and collaborative workflows (pull requests/code review) for configuration changes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a honeypot in network security?
options: A decoy system that lures attackers to study their techniques and divert them from real assets
questions.explanation: A honeypot is an intentionally vulnerable decoy system. Attackers targeting it reveal their methods, tools, and attack patterns while being distracted from real production systems.

questions.question: What is the Content-Type header value when sending JSON data in a REST API request?
questions.explanation: The HTTP Content-Type header `application/json` tells the server the request body is JSON-encoded data. `Accept: application/json` tells the server the client wants a JSON response.

questions.question: What is a Jinja2 template used for in network automation?
options: A templating language for generating configuration files with variable substitution
questions.explanation: Jinja2 is a Python templating engine used to generate network device configurations from templates + variable data. Ansible uses Jinja2 natively for config templates, enabling one template to configure many devices.

questions.question: What is the Cisco ACI (Application Centric Infrastructure) approach to SDN?
options: A policy-based data center SDN solution with a centralized APIC controller using an application-centric policy model
questions.explanation: Cisco ACI is a data center SDN solution where the APIC (Application Policy Infrastructure Controller) manages network policy using an object model. Policy is defined in terms of application requirements (Endpoints, EPGs, Contracts).

questions.question: What is the purpose of a digital signature?
options: Provides authentication and non-repudiation — proves the message came from the claimed sender and was not altered
questions.explanation: A digital signature (hash signed with sender's private key) proves authenticity (sender identity) and integrity (message not altered). The recipient verifies using the sender's public key. Provides non-repudiation.

questions.question: What is network segmentation and why is it a security best practice?
options: Combining all VLANs into one large network
Dividing the network into isolated zones (using VLANs, firewalls, ACLs) to limit lateral movement if one zone is compromised
questions.explanation: Segmentation limits blast radius — if an attacker compromises one zone (DMZ, guest VLAN), they can't easily pivot to other zones (production, finance). Zones are separated by firewalls/ACLs enforcing least-privilege access.

questions.question: What is the purpose of API versioning (e.g., /api/v1/, /api/v2/)?
options: Allow backward-compatible API changes without breaking existing clients using the older version
Provide separate APIs for different users
questions.explanation: API versioning allows the API to evolve (adding/changing endpoints) without breaking existing integrations. Clients can explicitly request a version. Older versions remain supported for a deprecation period.

questions.question: What is an Ansible role and why is it used?
options: A security privilege level in Ansible Tower
A reusable, structured way to organize playbook tasks, variables, and files for a specific function (e.g., OSPF config, VLAN setup)
A Python module for network devices
questions.explanation: Ansible roles organize automation content into a standard directory structure. Roles are reusable — write once, use across multiple playbooks. They promote modularity and consistency in large automation projects.

questions.question: What are NETCONF datastores?
options: Named repositories for configuration data: running (active config), startup (saved config), and candidate (work-in-progress, if supported)
questions.explanation: NETCONF defines standard datastores: running (currently active config), startup (config loaded on boot), and candidate (editable copy of config before committing). Not all devices support all datastores.

questions.question: What is the purpose of network function virtualization (NFV)?
options: Runs network functions (firewall, load balancer, IDS, NAT) as software on standard x86 servers instead of dedicated hardware appliances
questions.explanation: NFV decouples network functions from proprietary hardware, running them as Virtual Network Functions (VNFs) on COTS (commercial off-the-shelf) x86 servers. Reduces CapEx, improves agility and scalability.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Ansible and Puppet in terms of architecture?
options: Ansible requires Python; Puppet does not
Ansible is agentless (push/SSH); Puppet uses agents on managed nodes that pull config from the Puppet master (TCP 8140)
questions.explanation: Ansible: agentless, push-based via SSH, YAML playbooks, no master required. Puppet: agent-based, pull model — agents check in with Puppet master (TCP 8140) every 30 minutes, Ruby-based manifests.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a source-of-truth (e.g., NetBox, Nautobot) in network automation?
options: Provides a CLI backup for devices
Acts as an authoritative record of intended network state (IP addresses, VLANs, device inventory) that automation uses as input
questions.explanation: A Source of Truth (SoT) like NetBox provides a centralized, authoritative record of the intended network state. Automation tools query the SoT for device data, IP assignments, and topology to generate configurations.

questions.question: A REST API call returns HTTP 401. What does this mean and how is it resolved?
options: The resource doesn't exist — check the URL
Authentication failed — check API credentials, token validity, or authentication method
questions.explanation: 401 Unauthorized = authentication failure. Check: (1) Is the API token/key included in the Authorization header? (2) Has the token expired? (3) Is the correct auth method used (Bearer token, Basic auth, API key)?

questions.question: What is microsegmentation in a zero-trust network?
options: Dividing the network into very small security zones, enforcing policies between individual workloads or even containers — limiting east-west lateral movement
questions.explanation: Microsegmentation uses software-defined policies to isolate individual workloads, VMs, or containers. Even if an attacker breaches one segment, they cannot move laterally without traversing a policy enforcement point.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a security audit in network management?
options: Systematically review security controls, configurations, and logs to verify compliance with policies and identify vulnerabilities before attackers do
questions.explanation: Security audits examine: ACL rules, password policies, patch levels, unused accounts, unnecessary services, logging configuration, physical access controls, and compliance with standards (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001).

questions.question: What Python library provides a multi-vendor abstraction for network device management (NAPALM)?
options: NAPALM (Network Automation and Programmability Abstraction Layer with Multivendor Support)
questions.explanation: NAPALM (Network Automation and Programmability Abstraction Layer with Multivendor support) provides a unified Python API across multiple vendor OSes (IOS, NXOS, EOS, JunOS). Methods like `get_interfaces()` work on all platforms.

questions.question: What is Nornir used for in network automation?
options: A Python automation framework that runs tasks against an inventory of devices concurrently, without relying on a DSL like Ansible YAML
questions.explanation: Nornir is a pure-Python automation framework with an inventory system and task execution engine. Unlike Ansible (YAML-based DSL), all logic is in Python — giving more control and easier integration with other Python libraries.

questions.question: What is event-driven automation?
options: Running automation scripts on a fixed schedule
Automatically triggering automation actions in response to specific network events (interface down, threshold breach, syslog message)
Running automation only during maintenance windows
questions.explanation: Event-driven automation (e.g., using Cisco EEM, Ansible Event-Driven, or Stackstorm) monitors for events and automatically runs remediation scripts — reducing MTTR and human intervention.

questions.question: What is infrastructure as code (IaC) and what key benefit does it provide?
options: Managing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files, enabling version control, consistency, and repeatable deployments across environments
questions.explanation: IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation) defines infrastructure in code files checked into source control. Benefits: consistency (dev/test/prod identical), auditability (git history), repeatability (idempotent), and rapid reprovisioning.

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous API calls?
options: Synchronous uses TCP; asynchronous uses UDP
Synchronous blocks waiting for a response; asynchronous submits a request and continues — receiving a callback or polling for completion
questions.explanation: Synchronous: caller waits (blocks) until response arrives. Asynchronous: caller submits request, gets a job ID or webhook notification when complete — allows parallelism. DNA Center uses async APIs for long-running operations.

questions.question: What is rate limiting in REST API design?
options: Limiting the size of API responses
Restricting how many API requests a client can make in a time period — returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) when exceeded
A QoS feature for API traffic
questions.explanation: Rate limiting protects APIs from abuse and overload by capping requests per time window (e.g., 1000 requests/hour per API key). HTTP 429 Too Many Requests is returned when limits are exceeded. Clients should implement exponential backoff.

questions.question: What is an Ansible handler and when does it run?
options: A script that runs before all tasks
A task that runs only when notified by another task — used for actions like restarting a service after a config change
A task that always runs last
questions.explanation: Ansible handlers run only when explicitly notified by a task that made a change. Example: an Nginx config task notifies a 'restart nginx' handler. Handlers run once at the end of the play, even if notified multiple times.

questions.question: What is a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)?
options: A virtual private network between two data centers
A logically isolated section of a public cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP) where you launch resources in a virtual network you define — with control over IP addressing, subnets, routing, and security
questions.explanation: A VPC is a logically isolated virtual network within a public cloud. You define IP ranges (CIDR), subnets, route tables, internet gateways, and security groups — giving cloud resources a private networking environment.

questions.question: What is model-driven programmability and why is YANG central to it?
options: Using YANG data models as the contract between the management system and network device — the model defines exactly what data can be read/written, enabling interoperability across tools and vendors
questions.explanation: Model-driven programmability uses YANG models to formally define device configuration and state data. NETCONF/RESTCONF use YANG models to ensure all tools (Ansible, NSO, scripts) interact with devices in a structured, validated way.

questions.question: Why was TKIP deprecated despite being an improvement over WEP?
options: TKIP is based on the RC4 stream cipher which has known cryptographic weaknesses — WPA2 with AES/CCMP replaced it
questions.explanation: TKIP added per-packet key mixing to RC4-based WEP hardware. But RC4 itself has weaknesses, and TKIP attacks (Beck-Tews) were demonstrated. IEEE deprecated TKIP in 2012. WPA2-AES/CCMP or WPA3 are required.

questions.question: What is an API endpoint?
options: The physical server hosting the API
A specific URL + HTTP method representing one API operation (e.g., GET /api/v1/devices returns device list)
questions.explanation: An endpoint combines a URL path and HTTP method: GET /devices (list), GET /devices/{id} (one device), POST /devices (create), PUT /devices/{id} (update), DELETE /devices/{id} (remove). Each is a distinct operation.

questions.question: What is an Ansible playbook?
options: A YAML file with one or more plays defining which hosts to target and which tasks to execute on them
A Python script for network devices
questions.explanation: An Ansible playbook is YAML with plays. Each play: specifies a host/group, defines variables, and lists tasks (module invocations). Example: play targeting 'routers' group that configures OSPF, NTP, and banners.

questions.question: What is 'intent' in Intent-Based Networking?
options: A high-level business or policy goal expressed to the network (e.g., 'isolate guest from production') which IBN systems translate into device configurations automatically
questions.explanation: IBN (Cisco DNA Center) lets admins define what the network should do in business terms. The system translates intent into configs, continuously verifies compliance, and auto-remediates when devices drift from the intended state.

questions.question: What is the IKE (Internet Key Exchange) protocol used for?
